pillar.metaDescription: Confused about Medicaid long-term care eligibility in 2026? Get the income, asset, and care rules explained in plain English. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
pillar.h1: Medicaid Long-Term Care Eligibility: The 2026 Guide for Seniors
pillar.quickAnswer: To qualify for Medicaid long-term care in 2026, a single senior generally needs income under about $2,982/month and countable assets at or below $2,000, plus a documented need for nursing-home-level care. Limits vary by state. For free help, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
pillar.intro: Medicaid is the largest payer of long-term care in America, but the rules are notoriously complex and vary by state. This guide breaks down the three eligibility tests every senior must meet.
pillar.heroSubhead: Income limits, asset limits, the 5-year look-back, and spousal protections decoded for 2026 — with a free expert at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What are the three tests for Medicaid long-term care eligibility?
sections.body: Medicaid long-term care eligibility rests on three tests, and you must pass all three. First is the income test: in 2026 a single applicant generally must have monthly income at or below roughly $2,982 (300% of the SSI federal benefit rate), though limits differ by state and program. Second is the asset test: countable assets must usually fall at or below $2,000 for an individual. Third is the functional or medical test, meaning a doctor or state assessor confirms you need a nursing-home level of care or hands-on help with daily activities. Each state administers Medicaid differently, so the exact thresholds and the name of the program vary. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm your state's rules.

sections.h2: What counts as income and assets for Medicaid?
sections.body: Countable income includes Social Security, pensions, annuities, and most other regular money you receive. Countable assets include cash, bank accounts, stocks, bonds, and second properties. Some assets are exempt and do not count: your primary home (up to an equity limit, often if a spouse lives there), one vehicle, personal belongings, and prepaid irrevocable burial plans. Retirement accounts may or may not count depending on your state and whether they are in payout status. Because the line between countable and exempt assets is where most families make costly mistakes, it is worth a free call to 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) before you spend down or transfer anything.

sections.h2: Does Medicaid have a look-back period?
sections.body: Yes. Medicaid uses a 5-year (60-month) look-back period for long-term care in nearly every state. When you apply, the agency reviews all financial transactions for the prior five years. Any assets you gifted or sold for less than fair market value can trigger a penalty period of Medicaid ineligibility. The penalty length is calculated by dividing the gifted amount by your state's penalty divisor (the average monthly cost of nursing home care, which ranges from roughly $7,900 to over $14,000 depending on the state). Importantly, the IRS $19,000 annual gift exclusion does not protect you from Medicaid penalties. Plan early.

sections.h2: How does Medicaid protect the healthy spouse?
sections.body: Medicaid includes spousal impoverishment protections so the healthy spouse (the community spouse) is not left destitute. In 2026, the community spouse can keep a Community Spouse Resource Allowance between $32,532 (minimum) and $162,660 (maximum), in addition to the exempt home and car. The community spouse may also keep a Minimum Monthly Maintenance Needs Allowance of income, set between $2,643.75 and $4,066.50 per month in 2026. These figures change yearly and several states apply more generous limits. Married couples should never assume both spouses must spend down to $2,000. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand exactly what your household can protect.

sections.h2: How do I apply, and where can I get free help?
sections.body: You apply for Medicaid long-term care through your state Medicaid agency, your local Area Agency on Aging, or the federal portal at Medicaid.gov. You will need proof of income, five years of bank statements, asset documentation, and a medical assessment confirming your level of care need. The application is detailed and a single error can delay coverage for months. Many seniors also qualify for both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligibility), which changes how benefits coordinate. The fastest, no-cost way to get it right is to call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) and let a licensed specialist walk you through your state's process.

faqs.q: What is the income limit for Medicaid long-term care in 2026?
faqs.a: In 2026, a single senior applying for nursing home Medicaid generally must have monthly income at or below about $2,982, which equals 300% of the SSI federal benefit rate. Some states use lower limits and allow a Qualified Income Trust to qualify higher earners. Limits vary by state.

faqs.q: What is the asset limit for Medicaid long-term care?
faqs.a: Most states cap countable assets at $2,000 for a single applicant in 2026. Your primary home (within an equity limit), one car, personal items, and an irrevocable burial plan are typically exempt. Married couples can protect substantially more through spousal allowances. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for your state's figures.

faqs.q: Does Medicaid pay for nursing homes?
faqs.a: Yes. Medicaid is the single largest payer of nursing home care in the United States, covering room, board, and medical care for eligible seniors. You must meet income, asset, and medical-need requirements. Unlike Medicare, Medicaid covers long-term custodial nursing home stays, not just short-term rehab.

faqs.q: What is the Medicaid 5-year look-back period?
faqs.a: The look-back period is the 60 months before your Medicaid application, during which the agency reviews all asset transfers. Gifts or below-market sales during this window create a penalty period of ineligibility. The look-back applies to long-term care Medicaid in nearly every state. Planning ahead avoids penalties.

faqs.q: Can I qualify for Medicaid if I own a home?
faqs.a: Often yes. Your primary residence is usually an exempt asset for Medicaid eligibility, especially if a spouse or dependent lives there, up to a home-equity limit set by your state. However, Medicaid estate recovery may later seek repayment from the home. The rules are nuanced, so call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: Is Medicaid the same as Medicare?
faqs.a: No. Medicare is federal health insurance based on age (65+) or disability and covers limited skilled care. Medicaid is a joint federal-state program based on financial need and is the main payer for long-term custodial care. Many seniors qualify for both, called dual eligibility, which maximizes coverage.

faqs.q: Do Medicaid eligibility rules differ by state?
faqs.a: Yes, significantly. Medicaid is administered by each state within federal guidelines, so income limits, asset rules, program names, waiver options, and penalty divisors all vary. A figure that qualifies you in one state may disqualify you in another. Always confirm your state's specific rules before applying.

faqs.q: How can 1-800-MEDIGAP help with Medicaid eligibility?
faqs.a: 1-800-MEDIGAP connects seniors and families with licensed specialists who explain your state's Medicaid rules, help you understand spend-down and spousal protections, and guide you through applying. The call is free with no obligation. Dial 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to speak with someone today.

page.intro: Medicaid long-term care eligibility comes down to income, assets, and proven medical need. Here is how each test works in 2026.
page.heroSubhead: The three eligibility tests, what counts, and how to apply — plus free guidance at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What are the income and asset limits for 2026?
sections.body: For 2026, a single senior seeking Medicaid long-term care generally needs monthly income at or below roughly $2,982 (300% of the SSI federal benefit rate) and countable assets at or below $2,000. Married couples are treated differently: the community spouse can keep between $32,532 and $162,660 in assets plus a monthly income allowance. Exempt assets such as your home (within an equity cap), one car, and burial funds do not count. Because every state sets its own thresholds and some require a Qualified Income Trust for higher earners, confirm your numbers by calling 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: What is the medical or functional test?
sections.body: Beyond money, you must demonstrate a need for care. For nursing home Medicaid, a state assessor or physician confirms you require a nursing-home level of care, typically meaning you need substantial help with activities of daily living like bathing, dressing, eating, or transferring, or you have a cognitive impairment such as dementia. Home and community-based waiver programs use a similar functional test so you can receive care at home instead. This assessment is mandatory and separate from the financial review. A specialist at 1-800-MEDIGAP can explain how your state conducts it.

sections.h2: Why does the 5-year look-back matter?
sections.body: When you apply for long-term care Medicaid, the agency reviews 60 months of financial history. Gifts or below-market transfers in that window trigger a penalty period of ineligibility, calculated using your state's penalty divisor. Because penalties can last months or years, eligibility planning works best when started early, ideally five or more years before you expect to need care. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand how the look-back affects your situation.

faqs.q: How much income can I have and still qualify for Medicaid long-term care?
faqs.a: In 2026, a single senior generally needs monthly income at or below about $2,982 for nursing home Medicaid. States with lower limits often allow a Qualified Income Trust to redirect excess income and qualify. Married applicants follow different rules. Limits vary by state.

faqs.q: What assets are exempt from Medicaid eligibility?
faqs.a: Typically exempt assets include your primary home up to a state equity limit, one vehicle, household goods and personal effects, and an irrevocable prepaid burial plan. Countable assets like cash, savings, and investments must usually total $2,000 or less for a single applicant in 2026.

faqs.q: Does Medicaid long-term care cover home care?
faqs.a: Yes, through Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waivers. These programs let eligible seniors receive personal care, homemaker services, and adult day care at home instead of in a nursing facility. Waiver slots can be limited and waitlists exist. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to explore your state's options.

faqs.q: How long does Medicaid eligibility take to determine?
faqs.a: Most states must decide within 45 days, or up to 90 days if a disability determination is needed. Missing documents, look-back questions, or income trust requirements can extend the timeline. Submitting a complete application speeds approval. 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you prepare so nothing delays your coverage.

faqs.q: Can I get free help confirming my Medicaid eligibility?
faqs.a: Yes. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to speak with a licensed specialist who reviews your state's income, asset, and medical rules at no cost. They explain spousal protections, spend-down options, and the application steps so you can qualify with confidence.

children.keyword: does medicaid pay for assisted living

page.metaTitle: Does Medicaid Pay for Assisted Living? | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Does Medicaid pay for assisted living? It often covers care services but not room and board. See 2026 rules and call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for help.
page.h1: Does Medicaid Pay for Assisted Living?
page.quickAnswer: Medicaid does not pay assisted living room and board, but most states cover the care services inside assisted living through Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waivers. Coverage and waitlists vary by state. To find your options, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: Medicaid and assisted living have a partial relationship: care yes, rent usually no. Here is exactly what is and is not covered in 2026.
page.heroSubhead: What Medicaid covers in assisted living, what it does not, and how to apply — free help at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What does Medicaid cover in assisted living?
sections.body: In most states, Medicaid pays for the personal care and support services delivered inside an assisted living facility, not the housing itself. Covered services often include help with bathing, dressing, medication management, meals assistance, and supervision, typically funded through a Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waiver. What Medicaid generally does not cover is room and board, the monthly rent portion of assisted living. Some states cap that rent for Medicaid residents or pair it with an optional state supplement to Social Security. Because the gap varies widely, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to learn what your state pays.

sections.h2: How do you qualify and find a Medicaid assisted living facility?
sections.body: To use Medicaid in assisted living, you must meet your state's income and asset limits (generally about $2,982/month income and $2,000 in countable assets for a single senior in 2026) and a functional care-need test. You must also find a facility that accepts Medicaid waiver residents, because participation is voluntary and slots are limited. Many facilities accept only a fixed number of Medicaid residents or require you to private-pay first. A specialist at 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you identify Medicaid-friendly communities and confirm your eligibility before you move.

faqs.q: Why won't Medicaid pay assisted living room and board?
faqs.a: Federal Medicaid rules prohibit using Medicaid funds for room and board in assisted living, treating rent and meals as personal living costs. Medicaid covers the care services instead. Some states offset rent with an optional state supplement or set rate caps. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn your state's approach.

faqs.q: Which Medicaid program pays for assisted living care?
faqs.a: Most states fund assisted living care through Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waivers, which let Medicaid pay for support services outside a nursing home. Program names and benefits vary by state, and some have waitlists. 1-800-MEDIGAP can identify the right waiver for you.

faqs.q: Do all assisted living facilities accept Medicaid?
faqs.a: No. Accepting Medicaid is voluntary, so many assisted living communities take only private-pay residents or limit the number of Medicaid beds. Always confirm Medicaid acceptance before choosing a facility. 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you locate communities that participate in your state's waiver.

faqs.q: Can I afford assisted living rent on Medicaid?
faqs.a: Medicaid waiver residents typically use their Social Security income toward room and board, and some states add an optional state supplement to help cover the gap. The shortfall varies. A 1-800-MEDIGAP specialist can walk you through the math for your state and budget.

page.metaDescription: Medicaid nursing home eligibility requires income, asset, and care-level tests. See the 2026 rules in plain English. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: To qualify for Medicaid nursing home coverage in 2026, a single senior generally needs income at or below about $2,982/month, countable assets at or below $2,000, and a confirmed need for nursing-home-level care. Medicaid is the top payer for nursing homes. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: Medicaid pays for more nursing home care than any other source in America. Here is how to qualify in 2026.
page.heroSubhead: Income, asset, and medical rules for nursing home Medicaid — with free guidance at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What are the financial requirements for nursing home Medicaid?
sections.body: For 2026, a single senior generally must have monthly income at or below roughly $2,982 and countable assets at or below $2,000 to qualify for nursing home Medicaid. With nursing home costs averaging $8,000 to $9,000 or more per month, most residents who start private-pay eventually spend down to Medicaid eligibility. Married couples receive spousal protections: the community spouse can keep $32,532 to $162,660 in assets plus a monthly income allowance, so both spouses are not required to impoverish. Because spend-down and exemptions are easy to mishandle, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) before depleting savings.

sections.h2: What is the level-of-care requirement?
sections.body: Nursing home Medicaid requires proof that you need a nursing-home (or skilled) level of care. A state assessor evaluates whether you need ongoing help with activities of daily living such as eating, bathing, toileting, dressing, or transferring, or whether a cognitive condition like dementia makes 24-hour supervision necessary. This functional assessment is mandatory in addition to the financial tests. Meeting the medical standard but not the financial one (or vice versa) means you will not qualify. 1-800-MEDIGAP can explain how your state conducts the level-of-care assessment.

sections.h2: How does the look-back affect nursing home applicants?
sections.body: Nursing home Medicaid applicants face the strictest look-back scrutiny. The agency reviews 60 months of finances, and any gifts or below-market transfers create a penalty period of ineligibility during which you must pay privately. Because nursing home care is expensive, even a modest penalty can cost tens of thousands of dollars. Legitimate planning strategies exist, but they must be done correctly and ideally well before you apply. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand your options.

faqs.q: Does Medicaid cover the full cost of a nursing home?
faqs.a: Yes, for eligible residents Medicaid covers room, board, nursing care, and medical services in a participating facility. You contribute nearly all of your monthly income toward the cost (your patient pay amount), and Medicaid pays the rest. A small personal needs allowance is kept for you.

faqs.q: Will I lose my house if I go on Medicaid for a nursing home?
faqs.a: Not necessarily while you are alive, since your home is often an exempt asset. However, after death, Medicaid estate recovery may seek repayment from your estate, including the home. Protections exist for surviving spouses and certain dependents. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand the rules.

faqs.q: How much income do I keep on nursing home Medicaid?
faqs.a: Most of your monthly income goes toward your share of nursing home costs, but you keep a small personal needs allowance (often $50 to $200 depending on the state). A community spouse may also receive a monthly maintenance allowance from your income. Amounts vary by state.

faqs.q: Can Medicare pay for long-term nursing home care instead?
faqs.a: No. Medicare only covers short-term skilled nursing care, up to 100 days after a qualifying hospital stay, and not long-term custodial care. Medicaid is the program that pays for ongoing nursing home stays. Many seniors use Medicare first, then transition to Medicaid.

faqs.q: How do I start a nursing home Medicaid application?
faqs.a: Apply through your state Medicaid agency with proof of income, five years of financial records, and a medical assessment. The process is detailed and errors cause delays. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help preparing a complete, accurate application.

page.metaTitle: How to Apply for Medicaid for Elderly Parent | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Applying for Medicaid for an elderly parent? Follow this step-by-step 2026 guide on documents, eligibility, and the process. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: How to Apply for Medicaid for an Elderly Parent
page.quickAnswer: To apply for Medicaid for an elderly parent, gather proof of income, five years of bank statements, and asset and medical records, then apply through your state Medicaid agency or Medicaid.gov. Confirm eligibility first. For free step-by-step help, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: Helping a parent apply for Medicaid can feel overwhelming. This step-by-step guide simplifies the 2026 process for adult children.
page.heroSubhead: Documents, steps, and pitfalls when applying for a parent — plus a free helping hand at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What documents do you need to apply?
sections.body: Before applying, gather your parent's identification, Social Security and Medicare cards, proof of all income (Social Security, pensions, annuities), and 60 months of bank and financial statements to satisfy the look-back review. You will also need documentation of assets such as property deeds, vehicle titles, life insurance policies, and burial arrangements, plus medical records supporting their care need. If you act on your parent's behalf, have a power of attorney ready. Missing or incomplete paperwork is the top cause of delays. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for a complete checklist tailored to your state.

sections.h2: What are the steps to apply?
sections.body: First, confirm your parent meets the income, asset, and medical eligibility tests for your state. Second, choose the correct program, whether nursing home Medicaid, an HCBS waiver for home care, or dual Medicare-Medicaid coverage. Third, submit the application through your state Medicaid agency, local Area Agency on Aging, or Medicaid.gov. Fourth, complete the medical or functional assessment. Fifth, respond promptly to any requests for additional documents. The state typically decides within 45 days. Because a single error can restart the clock, many families call 1-800-MEDIGAP first to get it right the first time.

faqs.q: Can I apply for Medicaid on behalf of my parent?
faqs.a: Yes. An authorized representative, such as an adult child with power of attorney or a designated representative, can apply on a parent's behalf. You will sign forms confirming your authority. Having legal authority in place before applying prevents delays. 1-800-MEDIGAP can explain what authorization your state requires.

faqs.q: What if my parent's income is too high for Medicaid?
faqs.a: Many states allow a Qualified Income Trust (also called a Miller Trust) that redirects income above the limit so your parent can still qualify for long-term care Medicaid. The trust must be set up correctly and before approval. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn whether your state offers this.

faqs.q: How long does the Medicaid application take?
faqs.a: States generally must decide within 45 days, or up to 90 days when a disability determination is required. Look-back questions, income trusts, or missing documents can extend the timeline. Submitting a complete, accurate application is the best way to speed approval for your parent.

faqs.q: Do I need a lawyer to apply for Medicaid for my parent?
faqs.a: Not always. Straightforward cases can be handled directly, but complex situations involving look-back transfers, a home, or significant assets may benefit from professional guidance. Start with a free call to 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427), which can clarify whether your case needs specialized planning.

faqs.q: Where do I submit the Medicaid application?
faqs.a: Apply through your state Medicaid agency online or in person, your local Area Agency on Aging, or the federal portal at Medicaid.gov, which routes you to your state. The correct channel depends on the program you need. 1-800-MEDIGAP can direct you to the right place.

page.intro: Medicaid income limits for seniors depend on the program and your state. Here are the 2026 figures and what to do if you earn more.
page.heroSubhead: Long-term care vs. regular Medicaid limits, plus trust options for higher earners — free help at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What are the 2026 income limits?
sections.body: Income limits depend on which Medicaid program you need. For nursing home Medicaid and HCBS waivers, a single senior generally must have monthly income at or below about $2,982 in 2026 (300% of the SSI federal benefit rate). For regular aged Medicaid that helps with care at home, the limit is lower, around $994/month for an individual and $1,491 for a couple in roughly half the states. Married couples applying for long-term care are assessed individually, with income protections for the community spouse. Because states set different thresholds, confirm your figure by calling 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: What if your income is over the limit?
sections.body: Earning more than the limit does not automatically disqualify you. Many states are income-cap states that allow a Qualified Income Trust, also called a Miller Trust, which redirects income above the threshold into a special account used for care. Other states are medically needy states that let you spend down excess income on medical costs to qualify. Both paths have strict rules and must be set up correctly and on time. A licensed specialist at 1-800-MEDIGAP can tell you which approach your state uses and how to qualify despite higher income.

faqs.q: What is the Medicaid income limit for a single senior in 2026?
faqs.a: For long-term care Medicaid, a single senior generally needs monthly income at or below about $2,982 in 2026, which is 300% of the SSI federal benefit rate. Regular Medicaid uses a lower limit near $994/month in many states. Exact figures vary by state.

faqs.q: Does Social Security count toward the Medicaid income limit?
faqs.a: Yes. Social Security benefits, pensions, annuities, and most other regular income count toward Medicaid income limits. A small portion may be disregarded depending on the state and program. Knowing what counts is key, so call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review your specific income sources.

faqs.q: Can I qualify for Medicaid if my income is too high?
faqs.a: Often yes. Income-cap states allow a Qualified Income Trust to redirect excess income, while medically needy states let you spend down on medical bills to qualify. Both require careful setup. 1-800-MEDIGAP can identify which option your state offers and guide you through it.

faqs.q: Are Medicaid income limits different for married couples?
faqs.a: Yes. When one spouse needs long-term care, income is assessed individually, and the healthy community spouse may keep a monthly maintenance allowance between $2,643.75 and $4,066.50 in 2026. This prevents impoverishment. Married applicants should call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand spousal income rules.

faqs.q: Do Medicaid income limits change every year?
faqs.a: Yes. Most Medicaid income limits are tied to federal figures like the SSI benefit rate and poverty guidelines, which adjust annually. The 2026 numbers differ from prior years. Always verify current limits before applying, and call 1-800-MEDIGAP for the latest figures in your state.

page.metaTitle: Qualify for Medicaid for Nursing Home Care | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Learn how to qualify for Medicaid for nursing home care in 2026, including income, asset, spend-down, and look-back rules. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: How to Qualify for Medicaid for Nursing Home Care
page.quickAnswer: To qualify for Medicaid for nursing home care in 2026, meet your state's income limit (about $2,982/month for a single senior), reduce countable assets to $2,000 or less, pass the level-of-care assessment, and clear the 5-year look-back. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for guidance.
page.intro: Qualifying for nursing home Medicaid takes planning across four areas: income, assets, medical need, and the look-back. Here is the roadmap for 2026.
page.heroSubhead: Spend-down, spousal protections, and look-back strategy explained — with free help at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: How do you meet the income and asset tests?
sections.body: Start by comparing your income to your state's limit, generally about $2,982/month for a single senior in 2026. If you exceed it, a Qualified Income Trust may bridge the gap in income-cap states. Next, reduce countable assets to the $2,000 threshold through a legitimate spend-down, which can include paying off debt, prepaying funeral costs, making home repairs, or buying exempt items, never by gifting, which triggers penalties. Married couples protect far more thanks to spousal allowances. Because the line between smart spend-down and a penalized transfer is thin, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) before moving any money.

sections.h2: How do you pass the medical and look-back tests?
sections.body: Nursing home Medicaid requires a state assessment confirming you need a nursing-home level of care, meaning ongoing help with daily activities or supervision for a cognitive condition. Separately, the agency reviews 60 months of finances, and any uncompensated transfers create a penalty period of private-pay ineligibility. The penalty equals the gifted amount divided by your state's penalty divisor (often $7,900 to $14,000 per month). Effective qualifying strategies, like certain annuities or trusts, exist but must be executed correctly and early. 1-800-MEDIGAP can connect you with the guidance you need to qualify without costly mistakes.

faqs.q: What is a Medicaid spend-down for nursing home care?
faqs.a: A spend-down is reducing countable assets to the $2,000 limit by spending on permitted items, paying debt, prepaying funeral costs, or improving an exempt home, rather than gifting. Done correctly, it makes you eligible without penalty. 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you spend down the right way.

faqs.q: Can I give away assets to qualify for nursing home Medicaid?
faqs.a: No. Gifting or transferring assets for less than fair value within the 5-year look-back triggers a penalty period of ineligibility. The IRS gift exclusion does not apply to Medicaid. Legitimate planning exists, but timing is critical. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP before transferring anything.

faqs.q: How does the level-of-care assessment work?
faqs.a: A state assessor evaluates whether you need a nursing-home level of care, typically requiring substantial help with activities of daily living or 24-hour supervision for cognitive impairment. This functional test is mandatory alongside the financial tests. 1-800-MEDIGAP can explain how your state conducts it.

faqs.q: Will my spouse have to pay for my nursing home?
faqs.a: Not to the point of impoverishment. Spousal protections let the community spouse keep $32,532 to $162,660 in assets plus a monthly income allowance in 2026. Both spouses are not required to spend down. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn exactly what your spouse can protect.

faqs.q: When should I start planning to qualify for Medicaid?
faqs.a: As early as possible, ideally five or more years before you expect to need nursing home care, because of the look-back period. Early planning preserves more assets legally. Even if care is needed soon, options remain. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to start planning today.

page.intro: Medicaid eligibility figures update every year. Here are the current 2026 rules seniors and families need to know.
page.heroSubhead: Updated 2026 income, asset, and spousal numbers — with free expert help at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What changed for 2026?
sections.body: Medicaid eligibility numbers adjust yearly with federal benchmarks. For 2026, the long-term care income limit for a single senior is generally about $2,982/month, the countable asset limit remains $2,000, and spousal protections rose with the Community Spouse Resource Allowance now ranging from $32,532 to $162,660. The community spouse income allowance falls between $2,643.75 and $4,066.50 monthly. Penalty divisors, tied to rising nursing home costs, also increased in many states. Because these figures differ by state and update each year, confirm the current numbers for your situation by calling 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: Who qualifies for Medicaid as a senior in 2026?
sections.body: Seniors qualify based on the program they need. Long-term care Medicaid (nursing home or HCBS waiver) requires the income, asset, and care-level tests above. Regular aged Medicaid uses lower income limits, near $994/month for an individual, and helps with general health costs. Many seniors are dual eligible for both Medicare and Medicaid, which coordinates coverage and can eliminate most out-of-pocket costs. Eligibility is not one-size-fits-all, and the right program depends on your health and finances. A specialist at 1-800-MEDIGAP can match you to the program you qualify for in 2026.

faqs.q: What is the 2026 Medicaid asset limit for seniors?
faqs.a: In 2026, the countable asset limit for a single senior applying for long-term care Medicaid is generally $2,000. Exempt assets like your home, one car, and burial funds do not count. Married couples protect far more through the Community Spouse Resource Allowance. Limits vary by state.

faqs.q: What is the 2026 Medicaid income limit for seniors?
faqs.a: For 2026 long-term care Medicaid, a single senior generally needs income at or below about $2,982/month. Regular aged Medicaid limits are lower, near $994/month in many states. Higher earners may qualify using a Qualified Income Trust. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for your state's figure.

faqs.q: Did Medicaid eligibility rules change in 2026?
faqs.a: The structure stayed the same, but the dollar figures updated. For 2026, spousal asset and income allowances rose, and many states increased penalty divisors with higher nursing home costs. Always use current-year numbers when applying. 1-800-MEDIGAP can confirm the latest 2026 limits.

faqs.q: Can a senior have Medicare and Medicaid at the same time in 2026?
faqs.a: Yes. Seniors who meet Medicaid's financial limits while on Medicare are dual eligible, and the two programs coordinate so Medicaid covers costs Medicare does not, like long-term care and many out-of-pocket expenses. Dual eligibility maximizes coverage. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see if you qualify.

faqs.q: Where can I confirm my 2026 Medicaid eligibility?
faqs.a: Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to speak with a licensed specialist who reviews the current 2026 income, asset, and care rules for your state at no cost. They help you understand which program fits and how to apply correctly.

page.metaDescription: See 2026 Medicaid asset limits for seniors, what counts, what's exempt, and how couples protect more. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help.
page.quickAnswer: In 2026, the Medicaid asset limit for a single senior is generally $2,000 in countable assets. Your home (within an equity cap), one car, and burial funds are exempt, and a married community spouse can keep $32,532 to $162,660. Limits vary by state. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: Medicaid asset limits trip up many seniors, but exemptions and spousal rules protect more than most people realize. Here are the 2026 details.
page.heroSubhead: Countable vs. exempt assets and spousal protections explained — free help at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What counts toward the Medicaid asset limit?
sections.body: Countable assets include cash, checking and savings accounts, stocks, bonds, mutual funds, certificates of deposit, second homes, and additional vehicles. For a single senior in 2026, these must generally total $2,000 or less to qualify for long-term care Medicaid. Exempt (non-countable) assets include your primary residence up to a state home-equity limit, one vehicle, household goods and personal effects, an irrevocable prepaid burial plan, and certain life insurance with low face value. Retirement accounts may count depending on your state and payout status. Because misclassifying an asset can disqualify you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to review your specific holdings.

sections.h2: How do married couples protect assets?
sections.body: When only one spouse needs long-term care, federal spousal impoverishment rules protect the healthy community spouse. In 2026, the community spouse can keep a Community Spouse Resource Allowance between $32,532 and $162,660, on top of the exempt home and car. This means married couples do not have to spend everything down to $2,000. Several states use the higher maximum for all couples. Strategic, legal moves can further protect assets, but they must comply with the 5-year look-back. A 1-800-MEDIGAP specialist can explain exactly what your household is entitled to keep.

faqs.q: What is the Medicaid asset limit for a single senior in 2026?
faqs.a: In 2026, a single senior generally must have countable assets of $2,000 or less to qualify for long-term care Medicaid. Exempt assets like a primary home, one vehicle, and prepaid burial do not count toward this limit. Exact limits vary slightly by state.

faqs.q: Does my house count against the Medicaid asset limit?
faqs.a: Usually no. Your primary residence is typically exempt up to a state home-equity limit, especially if a spouse or dependent lives there. However, Medicaid estate recovery may later seek repayment from the home after death. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand the protections.

faqs.q: How much can a married couple keep for Medicaid?
faqs.a: In 2026, the community spouse can keep between $32,532 and $162,660 in countable assets through the Community Spouse Resource Allowance, plus the exempt home and car. This prevents impoverishment. Several states apply the higher maximum. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for your state's rule.

faqs.q: Are retirement accounts counted as Medicaid assets?
faqs.a: It depends on your state. Some states count IRAs and 401(k)s as assets, while others exempt them if they are in required-minimum-distribution payout status, treating the payments as income instead. Because the rule varies, call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn how your state treats retirement accounts.

faqs.q: Can I reduce assets to qualify for Medicaid?
faqs.a: Yes, through a legitimate spend-down, such as paying debt, prepaying funeral costs, or improving an exempt home, never by gifting, which triggers look-back penalties. Doing it correctly is essential. 1-800-MEDIGAP can guide you through a compliant spend-down for your state.

page.metaDescription: Medicaid HCBS waivers help seniors get care at home instead of a nursing facility. See 2026 eligibility and services. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waivers let eligible seniors receive long-term care at home or in assisted living instead of a nursing facility. You must meet income, asset, and nursing-home-level care tests; slots can be limited. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: HCBS waivers help seniors age in place by funding care outside a nursing home. Here is how they work and who qualifies in 2026.
page.heroSubhead: Covered services, eligibility, and waitlists explained — with free guidance at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What do HCBS waivers cover?
sections.body: Home and Community-Based Services waivers let Medicaid pay for long-term care delivered at home or in community settings rather than a nursing home. Covered services commonly include personal care assistance (bathing, dressing, grooming), homemaker and chore help, adult day care, home-delivered meals, respite care for family caregivers, home modifications like grab bars and ramps, and personal emergency response systems. The goal is to help seniors remain safely at home. Benefits and program names vary by state. To find out which services your state's waiver covers and whether you qualify, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: Who qualifies, and are there waitlists?
sections.body: To qualify for an HCBS waiver, a senior must meet the same income (about $2,982/month for a single applicant in 2026) and asset ($2,000) limits as nursing home Medicaid, plus a functional test confirming a nursing-home level of care need, even though care is provided at home. Unlike nursing home Medicaid, waivers are not an entitlement, so states cap enrollment and many maintain waitlists that can last months or longer. Applying early and getting on the list quickly matters. A 1-800-MEDIGAP specialist can help you apply and navigate any waitlist in your state.

faqs.q: What is a Medicaid HCBS waiver?
faqs.a: An HCBS (Home and Community-Based Services) waiver is a Medicaid program that pays for long-term care at home or in the community instead of a nursing home. It funds services like personal care, homemaker help, and adult day care for eligible seniors. Programs vary by state.

faqs.q: Who is eligible for an HCBS waiver?
faqs.a: Seniors who meet Medicaid's income and asset limits and require a nursing-home level of care can qualify for an HCBS waiver, even though care is delivered at home. Each state sets enrollment caps. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check your eligibility and your state's program.

faqs.q: Is there a waitlist for HCBS waivers?
faqs.a: Often yes. Because waivers are not a guaranteed entitlement like nursing home Medicaid, states limit the number of slots, and many have waitlists lasting months or longer. Applying early improves your chances. 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you get on your state's list quickly.

faqs.q: Can an HCBS waiver pay for assisted living?
faqs.a: In many states, yes. HCBS waivers can cover the care services portion of assisted living, though not room and board. This lets eligible seniors use Medicaid in an assisted living setting. Coverage varies by state. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn your options.

faqs.q: How do I apply for an HCBS waiver?
faqs.a: Apply through your state Medicaid agency or local Area Agency on Aging, providing financial documents and completing a functional assessment. The process mirrors nursing home Medicaid but adds waiver-specific steps. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help applying.

children.keyword: dual eligible medicare and medicaid seniors

page.metaTitle: Dual Eligible Medicare and Medicaid Seniors | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Dual eligible seniors get both Medicare and Medicaid, often cutting costs to near zero. See 2026 rules and benefits. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: Dual Eligible Medicare and Medicaid Seniors
page.quickAnswer: Dual eligible seniors qualify for both Medicare and Medicaid. Medicare pays first for hospital and medical care, and Medicaid covers what Medicare does not, including long-term care, premiums, and most out-of-pocket costs. This can reduce expenses to near zero. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: Being dual eligible for Medicare and Medicaid is one of the most valuable positions a senior can be in. Here is how the two programs work together in 2026.
page.heroSubhead: How Medicare and Medicaid coordinate, plus the savings dual status unlocks — free help at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: How do Medicare and Medicaid work together?
sections.body: When you are dual eligible, Medicare is the primary payer and Medicaid is secondary. Medicare covers hospital stays (Part A), doctor visits (Part B), and prescriptions (Part D), while Medicaid fills the gaps, paying your Medicare premiums, deductibles, copays, and coinsurance, plus services Medicare excludes such as long-term nursing home care, in-home support, and some dental, vision, and hearing care. The result is that most dual eligible seniors pay little to nothing out of pocket. Coordinating both programs correctly maximizes your benefits. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to make sure you are getting everything you are entitled to.

sections.h2: What are the levels of dual eligibility?
sections.body: Dual eligibility comes in two main forms. Full dual eligible seniors receive complete Medicaid benefits plus help with Medicare costs. Partial dual eligible seniors qualify for a Medicare Savings Program that pays some or all of their Medicare premiums and cost-sharing, but not full Medicaid services. There are also Dual Eligible Special Needs Plans (D-SNPs), Medicare Advantage plans built specifically to coordinate care for dual members, often with extra benefits. Which category fits you depends on your income and assets. A 1-800-MEDIGAP specialist can determine your level and enroll you in the right coverage.

faqs.q: What does it mean to be dual eligible?
faqs.a: Dual eligible means you qualify for both Medicare and Medicaid at the same time. Medicare pays first for medical care, and Medicaid covers remaining costs and services Medicare does not, like long-term care. This combination minimizes out-of-pocket expenses for low-income seniors.

faqs.q: What does Medicaid pay for dual eligible seniors?
faqs.a: For dual eligible seniors, Medicaid typically pays Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copays, plus services Medicare excludes such as long-term nursing home care, in-home personal care, and often dental, vision, and hearing. Benefits vary by state and dual eligibility level. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for details.

faqs.q: What is a Dual Eligible Special Needs Plan (D-SNP)?
faqs.a: A D-SNP is a type of Medicare Advantage plan designed for people with both Medicare and Medicaid. It coordinates benefits from both programs in one plan, often adding extras like transportation, over-the-counter allowances, and care coordination. 1-800-MEDIGAP can check if a D-SNP fits you.

faqs.q: Do dual eligible seniors pay premiums?
faqs.a: Usually not. Full dual eligible seniors typically have their Medicare Part A and Part B premiums paid by Medicaid, and they often qualify for full prescription drug subsidies. The result is little to no monthly cost. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm your premium coverage.

faqs.q: How do I become dual eligible?
faqs.a: If you already have Medicare, apply for Medicaid through your state agency. If you meet your state's income and asset limits, you become dual eligible and the programs coordinate automatically. 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) can review your finances and help you apply for free.

page.metaTitle: Medicaid Penalty Period for Gifting Assets | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Gifting assets can trigger a Medicaid penalty period of ineligibility. See how the 2026 look-back and penalty divisor work. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: Medicaid Penalty Period for Gifting Assets
page.quickAnswer: Gifting or transferring assets for less than fair value within Medicaid's 5-year look-back triggers a penalty period of long-term care ineligibility. The penalty equals the gifted amount divided by your state's penalty divisor (often $7,900 to $14,000/month). Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) before transferring assets.
page.intro: One well-meaning gift can cost months of Medicaid coverage. Here is how the penalty period for gifting assets works in 2026 and how to avoid it.
page.heroSubhead: The look-back, penalty math, and safe alternatives explained — with free help at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: How is the Medicaid penalty period calculated?
sections.body: When you apply for long-term care Medicaid, the agency reviews 60 months of finances. Any gift or below-market transfer in that window creates a penalty period during which you must pay for care yourself. The penalty length equals the total gifted amount divided by your state's penalty divisor, which reflects the average monthly cost of nursing home care (ranging from about $7,900 to over $14,000 depending on the state). For example, gifting $80,000 in a state with a $10,000 divisor creates an 8-month penalty. Crucially, the penalty does not start until you would otherwise qualify. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) before making any gift.

sections.h2: What gifts trigger a penalty, and what is safe?
sections.body: Almost any uncompensated transfer counts: cash gifts to children or grandchildren, paying a relative's bills, selling a home or car below market value, or forgiving a loan. Even small holiday or birthday gifts can be flagged. The IRS $19,000 annual gift tax exclusion does not protect you, because Medicaid has separate rules. Some transfers are exempt, such as transfers to a spouse, a disabled child, or a caregiver child who meets specific conditions. Because the line between penalized and exempt transfers is technical, never gift assets to qualify for Medicaid without first calling 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: What is the Medicaid penalty period for gifting assets?
faqs.a: The penalty period is a span of long-term care Medicaid ineligibility triggered by gifting or transferring assets for less than fair value within the 5-year look-back. Its length equals the gifted amount divided by your state's penalty divisor. During the penalty, you must pay for care privately.

faqs.q: How long does a Medicaid penalty last?
faqs.a: The penalty length depends on how much you gifted and your state's penalty divisor (the average monthly nursing home cost, roughly $7,900 to $14,000). For example, $50,000 gifted with a $10,000 divisor creates a 5-month penalty. There is no maximum cap on the penalty period.

faqs.q: Does the IRS gift exclusion protect me from Medicaid penalties?
faqs.a: No. The IRS $19,000 annual gift tax exclusion is a tax rule and has no bearing on Medicaid. Medicaid penalizes any uncompensated transfer within the 5-year look-back regardless of IRS limits. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP before gifting so you do not accidentally trigger a penalty.

faqs.q: Are any asset transfers exempt from the Medicaid penalty?
faqs.a: Yes. Transfers to a spouse, a blind or disabled child, a trust for a disabled person, or a caregiver child who lived with and cared for you for two years can be exempt. These exemptions are specific and technical. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see if a transfer qualifies.

faqs.q: Can I undo a gift to avoid a Medicaid penalty?
faqs.a: Sometimes. Returning the gifted assets (a cure) can reduce or eliminate the penalty in many states, but the rules are strict and timing matters. If you have already made a transfer, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) right away to explore your options before applying.
